title: Per-creator index / global swapId confusion lets callers withdraw other users' escrowed assets
claim: `swapMatch[swapId]` stores only the swap creator's local array index, but `closeSwapIntent` and `cancelSwapIntent` combine that local index with caller-supplied creator addresses while always moving assets from the global `nftsOne[_swapId]` / `nftsTwo[_swapId]` buckets. A user who has an opened swap at the same local index as a victim can satisfy the ownership/status checks against their own swap entry and operate on the victim swap's escrowed assets.
impact: Attackers can steal NFTs, ERC20s, ERC1155s, or CryptoPunks escrowed in another user's `nftsOne` bucket. The victim's actual swap record is left open or otherwise inconsistent, which can strand any ETH tracked in the victim's swap struct and make recovery impossible.
paths: Victim creates swap `V` at local index `i`, so `swapMatch[V] = i` and `nftsOne[V]` holds the victim's escrowed assets.
Attacker creates any opened swap at their own local index `i`.
Attacker calls `cancelSwapIntent(V)`.
The function validates `swapList[attacker][i]`, but transfers `nftsOne[V]` back to the attacker and marks the attacker's own swap entry cancelled.

title: User-controlled `typeStd` can route whitelisted assets through an unset custom bridge and bypass escrow entirely
claim: The contract whitelists only `dapp` addresses and trusts the caller-supplied `typeStd`. Any `typeStd` value other than the three hardcoded sentinels or `CRYPTOPUNK` falls into the custom-bridge branch, which blindly calls `dappRelations[dapp]` without checking that a relation is configured or that it is a real bridge contract. For an ordinary whitelisted token with no bridge relation, these calls can succeed while moving no assets.
impact: A maker or taker can advertise escrow that was never deposited, then receive the counterparty's real NFTs/ERC20s/ETH while their own side transfers nothing.
paths: A standard token/collection is whitelisted, but `dappRelations[dapp]` is unset or points to a non-bridge address.
Attacker creates a swap using that whitelisted `dapp` but supplies an arbitrary nonstandard `typeStd`, forcing the custom bridge path.
`createSwapIntent` records the swap even though the attacker's asset transfer is a no-op.
A victim closes the swap; their side transfers normally, while the attacker's side again uses the no-op bridge path and delivers nothing.

title: ERC20 transfers are treated as successful even when the token signals failure
claim: The custom ERC20 interface omits the standard boolean return values, and every ERC20 `transfer` / `transferFrom` call ignores success. Tokens that fail by returning `false` instead of reverting therefore appear to the contract as successful deposits, payouts, or refunds.
impact: A maker can create a swap whose ERC20 escrow was never actually deposited, or a taker can receive the maker's escrowed assets without paying the requested ERC20 consideration. Failed ERC20 refunds during cancellation can likewise leave users with a cancelled swap but no returned tokens.
paths: Victim creates a swap that requires a whitelisted ERC20 in `nftsTwo`, or relies on an ERC20 in `nftsOne` being escrowed.
Attacker closes the swap without a real balance/allowance, using a token that returns `false` on failure.
The `transferFrom` call at line 249 returns `false`, but `BatchSwap` continues executing.
The victim's escrowed assets and ETH transfer anyway, even though the ERC20 payment never occurred.

title: `cancelSwapIntent` is reentrant through token or bridge callbacks before the swap is marked cancelled
claim: `cancelSwapIntent` performs external token and bridge calls, and refunds ETH, before it sets `swapEnd` and `status = Cancelled`. If the swap creator is a contract and one of its whitelisted assets or bridge endpoints reenters during the loop, nested `cancelSwapIntent` calls will still see the swap as `Opened` and can withdraw `swapFee` and `valueOne` repeatedly from the contract's shared ETH balance.
impact: A malicious swap creator can drain ETH that belongs to other users' swaps, leaving the contract insolvent and causing unrelated settlements or cancellations to fail.
paths: Attacker creates a swap from a contract address and includes a malicious whitelisted token or bridge-controlled asset in `nftsOne`.
Attacker calls `cancelSwapIntent` through that contract.
During the external transfer at lines 281-296, the malicious token/bridge reenters `cancelSwapIntent` for the same swap before line 303 executes.
Each nested call refunds `swapFee` and `valueOne` again, draining pooled ETH from the contract.

title: Using Solidity `transfer` for ETH payouts can permanently brick swaps involving contract recipients
claim: All ETH payouts use Solidity's `transfer`, which hardcodes a 2300-gas stipend and reverts if the recipient is a contract with a nontrivial or intentionally reverting receive/fallback path. This affects fee forwarding, settlement payouts, and cancellation refunds.
impact: Swaps that involve contract wallets, proxies, vaults, or other recipients that cannot accept a 2300-gas `transfer` can become uncloseable or uncancellable, permanently locking escrowed NFTs and ETH until the affected address is changed, if that is even possible.
paths: A swap creator, counterparty, or configured fee vault is a contract whose receive/fallback path reverts or needs more than 2300 gas.
`closeSwapIntent` or `cancelSwapIntent` reaches one of the `transfer` calls.
The ETH transfer reverts, which reverts the entire close/cancel attempt.
Escrowed assets remain locked in `BatchSwap`, and some address combinations cannot be repaired on-chain.
